content_html:
INF10025 Data Management and AnalyticsLecture 10 – Part 3
JSON

Searching for data in a NOSQL database
Can you guess which language you use to search a NoSQL database?
If you said SQL – you are Correct!!!!
That's right , SQL is used to search a NoSQL database.
Actually it’s called DocumentDB SQL.
It is a subset of the normal SQL standard.
Remember:
A NoSQL database really means A Non Relational database
NoSQL actually stands for Not Only SQL

Query Example
Students can create a free Azure login via DreamSparkhttps://catalog.imagine.microsoft.com/en-us/Catalog/Product/99
There you can create your own DocumentDB
However, Microsoft have also provided a free DocumentDB Query Playground
You use an existing database (they provide it) and you can run test queries etc.

The Family collection
One of the collections is called FAMILY.  Some of the properties stored in the  Documents are:
FAMILY
id
lastname
parents
givenName    OR
firstName
familyName
children
givenName
gender
grade
pets
givenName
address
state
county
city

Query Example
Click the > symbol to scroll to next result
Use SQL to retrieve data in the same manner as you would with a RDBMS

Playground Bugs / Issues
There seems to be a BUG in the Azure DocumentDB  Query Playground
(or more likely I am not using 100% correctly)
Queries for the Family collection seems to also retrieve data from the Food collection.
All Food documents have an id of 5 digits
All Family documents have an id longer than 5 digits
So many examples below have the following where clause	WHERE LENGTH(f.id) > 5
Also some documentation says FAMILY and some says FAMILIES.
Both seem to work identically

Using aliases instead of the table name
SELECT	f.id
FROM		Families f
WHERE	 LENGTH(f.id)>5
6 Results
"AndersenFamily"
"MeyerAndFamily"
"TheSmiths"
"TheAlexanders"
"WakefieldFamily"
"KinDocument" (This document doesn't seem to contain any family data)

Using the ID in the Where clause
If you know the id of a document, you can use that in the WHERE clause
SELECT  	f.id
FROM		Families f
WHERE	 f.id = "AndersenFamily"
1 Results
"AndersenFamily"
It is better to use the UPPER function
WHERE	UPPER(f.id) = "ANDERSENFAMILY"

Retrieve multiple columns
SELECT f.id, f.lastName, f.parents
FROM Families f
WHERE LENGTH(f.id)>5
6 Results
You can see that the properties found in each document differ slightly

STARTSWITH() & CONTAINS () functions
SELECT f.id, f.lastName, f.parents
FROM Families f
WHERE LENGTH(f.id)>5
AND STARTSWITH(f.id, "The")       retrieves 3 & 4
SELECT f.id, f.lastName, f.parents
FROM Families f
WHERE LENGTH(f.id)>5
AND STARTSWITH(UPPER(f.id), "THE")   retrieves 3 & 4
SELECT f.id, f.lastName, f.parents
FROM Families f
WHERE LENGTH(f.id)>5
AND CONTAINS(f.id, "M")     retrieves 2
SELECT f.id, f.lastName, f.parents
FROM Families f
WHERE LENGTH(f.id)>5
AND CONTAINS(UPPER(f.id), "M")     retrieves 1, 2, 3 & 5
Data
"AndersenFamily"
"MeyerAndFamily"
"TheSmiths"
"TheAlexanders"
"WakefieldFamily"
"KinDocument"
1
2
3
4
5
6

INDEX_OF () function
SELECT f.id, f.lastName, f.parents
FROM Families f
WHERE LENGTH(f.id)>5
AND INDEX_OF(UPPER(f.id), "FAMILY")>=0
3 Results
"AndersenFamily"
"MeyerAndFamily"
"TheSmiths"
"TheAlexanders"
"WakefieldFamily"
"KinDocument"
The INDEX_OF function returns the starting position of a string within a larger string.
This example searches for the text "Family" within the f.id property.
If not found the function returns -1
INDEX_OF("ANDERSENFAMILY","FAMILY")
returns a value of 8.
INDEX_OF("ANDERSENFAMILY","A")
returns a value of 0.
INDEX_OF("ANDERSENFAMILY","ND")
returns a value of 1.
INDEX_OF("ANDERSENFAMILY","DER")
returns a value of 2.

Properties and Aliases
When you use a property in a SQL statement it can be written as	style # 1:  alias.propertyname         or
style # 2 : alias["propertyname"]            Note: the dot is removed
SELECT  f.id,   f["givenName"] FROM family f  WHERE LENGTH["id"]) > 5
If a property in a document contains a space, then you MUST use style # 2
SELECT v.id,   v["volcano name"] FROM volcano v

Column Aliases
When executing this query:
SELECT  f.id,   f.givenName FROM family f
The result is:
{ "id": "TheAlexanders",
"givenName": "David"
}
These results property names can be brief or not supply enough information.
An alias can be used on each column name using the AS keyword
SELECT  f.id AS familyName,   f.givenName AS parentGivenName…
The result is:
{ "familyName": "TheAlexanders",
"parentGivenName": "David"
}

Nested Objects
A JSON document can contain properties, arrays and objects
SELECT f.id, f.lastName, f.address
FROM Families f
WHERE LENGTH(f.id)>5
This result returns an addressobject which has 3 properties
You may refer to the address object and it's properties in the SQL statementin a nested format
SELECT f.id, f.lastName, f.address.state,             f.address.county
FROM Families f
WHERE LENGTH(f.id)>5

Treating arrays as sub-documents
A JSON document can contain properties, arrays and objects
SELECT f.id, f.lastName, f.parents
FROM Families f
WHERE LENGTH(f.id)>5
parents contains an array [ ] of values
An array of values may be treatedas a sub-document (think a M:1 relationship)
The sub-document may be included in the FROM clause and requires a JOIN clause
SELECT f.id, f.lastName, p.firstName
FROM Families f
JOIN p IN f.parents
WHERE LENGTH(f.id)>5

Create JSON data
If you want to practice creating JSON documents then you can visit https://www.mockaroo.com/
This site allows you to freely create a JSON document with up 1000 entries

DocumentDB referecnes
https://azure.microsoft.com/en-in/documentation/articles/documentdb-sql-query/
https://www.documentdb.com/sql/tutorial
https://www.tutorialspoint.com/documentdb_sql/
